data.title: Is Grindr Still a Smart Pick After Its 16% Price Surge This Week?
data.description:
Are you watching Grindr’s stock and wondering whether it’s a smart buy right now? You are not alone. The company’s shares have taken some notable turns lately, and with every big move comes a new wave of investor questions. Just this past week, Grindr’s price surged by 16.2%, more than erasing some of the month’s earlier losses. Longer-term performance stands out with a 14.7% gain over the past year and an impressive 44.3% rise over three years. Yet, it’s not all smooth sailing, as the year-to-date return still sits at -16.1%, serving as a reminder of both the risks and the opportunities in play.

Behind these numbers, recent headlines have spotlighted shifting attitudes toward digital privacy and platform moderation. These are issues at the core of Grindr’s business model and public image. Updates in user safety features and evolving regulatory conversations have brought new attention to the company and may be shifting how the market values its future growth potential.

Based on a quick valuation checklist, Grindr scores a 3 out of 6 for being undervalued, indicating there are several areas where the stock might still be trading below its fair value. But is that the whole story? Next, we’ll unpack what those valuation checks mean and dig into how each approach stacks up for Grindr. If you are after a truly complete view on what this company is worth, stick around. We will go one layer deeper by the end of this analysis.

Why Grindr is lagging behind its peers

Advertisement

Approach 1: Grindr Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates a company’s intrinsic value by extrapolating future cash flow projections and then discounting them back to their present value. For Grindr, this approach centers on its ability to generate free cash flow over time and the growth analysts expect in the years ahead.

Currently, Grindr’s Free Cash Flow stands at $113.8 Million. According to analysts, this figure is projected to climb steadily, reaching $330.1 Million by 2029. After that, Simply Wall St’s model extrapolates growth even further, with estimates over $500 Million by 2035. The first five years are generally considered the most reliable forecasts.

Based on these projections, the DCF model assigns Grindr an intrinsic value of $37.29 per share. Compared to where shares trade now, this implies the stock is trading at a 59.6% discount. In other words, the market could be underestimating Grindr’s cash-generating potential if these assumptions hold true.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Grindr is undervalued by 59.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover more undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: Grindr Price vs Sales

For tech-driven and growth companies like Grindr, the Price-to-Sales (PS) ratio is a preferred valuation measure, especially when earnings are volatile or negative. This metric allows investors to assess how much they are paying for each dollar of revenue, which is useful in industries where profits can fluctuate as companies reinvest for growth.

Growth expectations and risk play a big role in what constitutes a "normal" or "fair" PS ratio. Fast-growing companies, or those with unique platforms and strong user bases, often command higher multiples. Riskier or slower-growing firms will tend to trade at lower ratios.

Currently, Grindr trades at a PS ratio of 7.31x. For context, this is above the Interactive Media and Services industry average of just 1.37x, and also ahead of the peer group average at 6.86x. At first glance, this premium might suggest that Grindr is expensive relative to its category.

This is where Simply Wall St's proprietary "Fair Ratio" comes in. The Fair Ratio for Grindr is 3.17x, which is calculated based on factors specific to Grindr, including growth prospects, profit margins, market capitalization, and company-specific risks. Rather than relying on a simple industry or peer comparison, the Fair Ratio offers a more nuanced view tailored to Grindr's unique circumstances.

Comparing Grindr’s actual PS ratio of 7.31x to its Fair Ratio of 3.17x, the stock appears overvalued on this basis. The premium is significantly above what would be suggested by its fundamentals and risk profile, according to this framework.

Result: OVERVALUED
